name: Resume Callback Study
description: Job callback rates by perceived race of applicant name. n=4,870. IMS Ch. 9.
studyDescription: A randomized field experiment in which 4,870 fictitious resumes were sent in response to job ads in Boston and Chicago. Resumes were randomly assigned names perceived as white-sounding or Black-sounding. Whether the applicant received a callback was recorded for each resume.

variableDescriptions.group: Perceived race based on the name on the resume: white or black
variableDescriptions.outcome: Whether the applicant received a callback: callback or no callback

sourceDetail: OpenIntro IMS Ch. 9. Original study: Bertrand, M. & Mullainathan, S. (2004). Are Emily and Greg More Employable Than Lakisha and Jamal? A Field Experiment on Labor Market Discrimination. American Economic Review, 94(4), 991-1013.

context.nullClaim: callback rate is the same regardless of perceived race

inferenceContexts.parameter: the difference in callback rates between resumes with white-sounding and Black-sounding names
inferenceContexts.claim: resumes with white-sounding names receive a higher callback rate than resumes with Black-sounding names
